blocks.text: You write posts as JSON files, and the page builds itself.
This is an **info** callout. Use it for general information or context.
This is a **tip** callout. Helpful advice or best practices go here.
This is a **warning** callout. Something to be careful about.
This is an **error** callout. Critical issues or mistakes to avoid.
This content is **hidden** until you click the header. Uses native `<details>` element, no JavaScript needed.
This one starts expanded because `open: true` was set.
Getting Started

blocks.content: This is the opening paragraph. The JSON-powered blog system lets you write content as simple JSON files (fn:1). No database, no CMS, no build step — just flat files on a server. You can use *formatting*, **bold text**, ==highlights==, and `inline code` anywhere in your content.

Here is the second paragraph with another reference to the first footnote (fn:1) to test multiple citations to the same note. It also introduces the second footnote (fn:2) which explains how the block system works.

blocks.text: The details are not the details. They make the design.
Formatting & Footnotes

blocks.caption: The Gaussian integral — a fundamental result in probability.

blocks.content: The third paragraph dives deeper into formatting options. You can use ~~strikethrough~~ for crossed-out text, ++underline++ for emphasis, and ||spoiler|| for hidden content (fn:3). H~2~O shows subscript, and 10^6^ shows superscript.

blocks.caption: City skyline
blocks.text: This image uses the **left** layout with text beside it. The text supports *formatting* and footnotes (fn:3). On mobile the layout stacks vertically.

blocks.content: This is the fourth paragraph. It references the formatting footnote again (fn:3) to verify that the backlink updates correctly between occurrences. It also mentions the architecture footnote (fn:4) for the first time.

Architecture & Extensibility
The only way to do great work is to love what you do. If you haven't found it yet, keep looking. Don't settle.
This **right** layout image has a `small` size. The image sits on the right and text wraps on the left. Great for screenshots with descriptions.

blocks.content: The fifth paragraph contains a reference to the architecture footnote (fn:4) a second time, plus a custom link: {click here|https://example.com}. Bare URLs like https://example.com are auto-linked automatically by the system.

Paragraph six is intentionally long to test responsive wrapping. The JSON-powered blog system uses a block-based architecture where each block is rendered independently. This makes it easy to add new block types without modifying existing code (fn:5). The system also supports footnotes with full formatting support.

The seventh paragraph references the extensibility footnote (fn:5) again to verify multi-reference behavior across distant paragraphs. Clicking this superscript and then using the backlink should return you exactly here.

blocks.text: Advanced Usage

blocks.content: Paragraph eight references the first footnote one more time (fn:1) and also the second footnote (fn:2). Three different footnotes in one paragraph to really test the backlink targeting.

This is the ninth paragraph. It only contains a single reference to the architecture footnote (fn:4) for the third time. By now you should have enough footnotes to properly test the backlink behavior.

The tenth and final paragraph wraps things up. The JSON-powered blog system is designed to be simple yet powerful (fn:5). Just write JSON files, register them in posts.json, and the page builds itself. No build step, no database, no CMS.

footnotes.text: The JSON-powered blog system uses a block-based architecture. Each post is a JSON file with a blocks array containing typed block objects.
Blocks are rendered by the renderBlock function which switches on the block type. Adding a new block type just requires a new case statement.
Formatting is processed by formatText() which converts syntax like **bold**, *italic*, ==highlight==, and `inline code` into HTML tags.
The architecture is simple: blog.html loads posts.json, which lists all post files. Clicking a post fetches its JSON and renders the blocks.
Extensibility is built in. New block types can be added by creating a case in renderBlock() and adding matching CSS styles.
